metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a git commit/push step to the repair plan: specify whether per-batch commit (after each <=200-ticket batch, `git add -A && git commit -m 'a3-remediation: batch N'`) or per-ticket commit is used; confirm rollback via `git reset --hard` works because all repairs are in git history.
findings.finding: The plan specifies per-ticket atomic file operations (os.rename for .retired, os.rename for <snap>.tmp over the snapshot) but never addresses the git commit/push step after repairs. The tracker is a git-backed worktree; uncommitted file changes are not durable — a git fetch+reset by another clone, or the rollback via `git reset --hard pre-a3-remediation`, would silently undo all repaired-but-uncommitted changes. The backup tag points to a git commit, not to OS filesystem state, so the rollback mechanism only works if repairs are themselves committed. The Scope section lists fsck.py and compact.py but no git staging/commit logic; no AC mentions a commit step.
findings.location: ## Scope / ## Acceptance Criteria / ## Safety
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Scope and AC4: after each batch of per-ticket repairs, stage and commit: `git -C <tracker> add -A && git -C <tracker> commit -m 'a3-remediation: batch N/M (SNAPSHOT_INCONSISTENT + ORPHAN)'`, then push. The per-ticket .a3-repaired markers can be untracked (gitignored) so they survive a git reset --hard, or they can be committed alongside the repairs. Document which.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the `_commands/compact.py (retire-still-present path)` Scope entry: either specify a new `retire_source_file(ticket_dir, filename)` helper that A3 adds to compact.py (with its API and callers named), or redirect the SNAPSHOT_INCONSISTENT repair to call A1's `RETIRED_SUFFIX` rename directly from fsck.py without going through compact.py.
findings.finding: The Scope section lists `_commands/compact.py (retire-still-present path)` as an A3 edit target, but the 'retire-still-present' rename for SNAPSHOT_INCONSISTENT repair (retiring individual source files named in a SNAPSHOT's source_event_uuids that are still on disk) is a distinct operation from what A1's compact.py rename does. A1's rename fires only during a compaction run on files compact.py itself folds; it does not provide a function for retiring an arbitrary named source file referenced by an existing SNAPSHOT. Current compact.py has no such path. The plan's prose says 'Repair = retire (`.retired`, via A1) each still-present listed source' but A1's compact.py change is not a callable retire-by-uuid helper — it is a behavioural change inside the compaction loop. The Scope label is unverifiable as written: either A3 needs to add its own per-file rename helper (making `compact.py` a genuine A3 edit target with specified new logic), or the scope entry is mislabeled.
findings.location: Scope section: `_commands/compact.py (retire-still-present path)` + Repair per fault class: SNAPSHOT_INCONSISTENT
findings.suggested_fix: Add an explicit `retire_source_file(ticket_dir: str, filename: str) -> None` helper to compact.py (or a shared util) that performs `os.rename(path, path + RETIRED_SUFFIX)` with the same error handling as A1's loop. Name it in the Scope section and as a call site in fsck --repair's SNAPSHOT_INCONSISTENT branch.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the Safety section whether `.a3-repaired` markers are committed to git (so hard-reset removes them) or untracked (requiring an explicit `find <store> -name .a3-repaired -delete` step as part of the rollback procedure), and add that cleanup step to the documented rollback sequence.
findings.finding: The `.a3-repaired` marker files' git-tracked vs. untracked status is unspecified. If untracked (i.e., never committed), a git hard-reset rollback leaves stale markers on disk; a subsequent re-run would incorrectly skip tickets whose repairs were rolled back, silently leaving the store in a corrupted state.
findings.location: Safety section — idempotent/resumable paragraph; Safety section — rollback paragraph
findings.suggested_fix: Commit markers as part of the per-ticket atomic operation (same git commit as the repaired snapshot), so git hard-reset atomically removes them. Add to the rollback procedure: 'After reset --hard, verify no .a3-repaired files remain (git status / find).' If markers must remain untracked, add an explicit rollback step: 'find <tracker> -name .a3-repaired -delete' before any re-run.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify a technical enforcement mechanism for the writer hold-off (e.g., temporarily set the remote branch to protected/read-only, or add a pre-receive hook), AND document the recovery path when `force-with-lease` is rejected on rollback (e.g., verify-then-force-push after confirming the intervening commit is safe to discard).
findings.finding: The dual-write window relies solely on a social/documentation hold-off for manual writers with no technical enforcement mechanism. If a manual push occurs during the repair window and a rollback is then needed, `git push --force-with-lease` will FAIL (because the remote tip moved), leaving the operator in an unspecified failure mode without a documented recovery path.
findings.location: Safety section — 'manual writers must hold off during the window'; AC line 3 'Reconciler paused … manual writers held off during the window'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Safety section: 'Before starting the repair run, set the tracker remote branch to protected (no direct pushes) via the hosting provider's API or a pre-receive hook that rejects non-repair commits; remove protection after the run completes or is rolled back. If force-with-lease is rejected on rollback, inspect the intervening commit(s): if they are repair artifacts only, force-push anyway; if they are user data writes, compensating-forward is mandatory (DATA LOSS RISK — do not force-push).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the concrete programmatic action for MISSING_CREATE in `--repair` mode (e.g., log a WARN, write a `.a3-needs-human` marker, skip the ticket, exclude from the 'zero faults' count, and include in the final report as UNRESOLVED) so that AC5's fixture test and AC6's 'zero UNRESOLVED faults' claim are objectively testable.
findings.finding: The MISSING_CREATE fault class is assigned 'human triage' with no programmatic behavioral specification for what `fsck --repair` does when it encounters one at runtime — no indication of whether the tool logs-and-skips, emits an interactive prompt, writes a special marker, or aborts the batch.
findings.location: ## Repair per fault class — MISSING_CREATE bullet; ## Acceptance Criteria AC5
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the MISSING_CREATE bullet: 'In --repair mode: log WARN + write `<ticket_dir>/.a3-needs-human` marker + include in final report as UNRESOLVED-HUMAN; excluded from the zero-faults gate (counts as triaged-resolved per AC6).'

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit pre-flight check in the A3 repair script that verifies `include_retired` is accepted by `reduce_ticket` (e.g. inspect its signature or call it with a no-op fixture) before starting the live repair batch, to gate on A2 being deployed.
findings.finding: The plan asserts that A2 (glandulous-potent-waxwing) 'adds the `include_retired` rebuild path in `reducer/_api.py`' and that A3 consumes it. Probe of the A2 ticket confirms A2 does specify adding `include_retired: bool = False` to both `reduce_ticket` (`reducer/_api.py`) and `prepare_event_files` (`reducer/_cache.py`). However, current `prepare_event_files` at line 97 uses `f.endswith('.json')` which NATURALLY excludes `.retired` files (they end in `.json.retired`), and line 102 uses `glob.glob('*.json')` which also excludes `.json.retired`. The `include_retired=True` path therefore requires adding a SEPARATE file-listing branch that explicitly includes `*.json.retired` files — non-trivial new logic. The A3 plan's ORPHAN_EVENT repair says 'A2's rebuild path (`reduce_ticket(..., include_retired=True)`) -> re-write snapshot' and treats this as settled. But if A2 is not yet deployed when A3 runs, the ORPHAN_EVENT repair has no implementation. The plan's 'depends_on A1 + A2 (wired)' claim is verifiable but the 'wired' status cannot be confirmed — A2's ticket status is 'open'.
findings.location: Why section: 'NOTE the sibling deliverables this consumes are NEW work... A2 (glandulous-potent-waxwing) adds the `include_retired` rebuild path in `reducer/_api.py`'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit pre-flight check to `fsck --repair` that verifies `reduce_ticket` accepts `include_retired` (e.g., inspect its signature or call a canary), and fail-fast with a clear error if A2 is not yet deployed, rather than silently skipping all ORPHAN_EVENT tickets.
findings.finding: The ORPHAN_EVENT repair path depends on `reduce_ticket(..., include_retired=True)` (A2's deliverable), but no fallback or pre-condition check is specified for what `fsck --repair` must do if A2 has not yet been merged when A3's repair script is invoked.
findings.location: ## Repair per fault class — ORPHAN_EVENT bullet; ## Safety — idempotent+resumable

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a synthetic-fixture test for the resumability invariant: simulate a partial run (some tickets marked `.a3-repaired`, some not), re-run the repair, and assert (a) already-marked tickets are skipped unchanged, (b) unmarked tickets are repaired, and (c) the final store state is identical to a single full run.
findings.finding: The idempotency/resumability behavior (re-run skips tickets with an existing `.a3-repaired` marker) is specified as a functional AC but is absent from the test AC — no test verifies that a partial run followed by a second run produces the correct final state without double-processing.
findings.location: AC line 4: 'Idempotent/resumable via per-ticket .a3-repaired markers (swept at the end)'; AC line 5 (test AC)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit preflight check (in the script or as an AC gate) that imports/invokes A1's RETIRED_SUFFIX and A2's include_retired parameter and fails fast with a clear error message if either is unavailable before any writes begin.
findings.finding: The deploy ordering of A1 → A2 → A3-repair is stated as a dependency but no preflight check is specified in the plan or ACs. If A3's repair script is invoked before A2 (include_retired rebuild path) is deployed, the ORPHAN_EVENT repair branch will import-error or silently no-op, leaving 11 orphan events unrepaired with no clear operator signal.
findings.location: Scope section — 'Consumes A1's .retired + A2's include_retired' / 'depends_on A1 + A2 (wired)' / AC line 5
findings.suggested_fix: At script startup, do `from reducer._api import reduce_ticket; import inspect; assert 'include_retired' in inspect.signature(reduce_ticket).parameters` (and equivalent for A1's RETIRED_SUFFIX) and abort with an actionable message if the assertion fails.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how the pass-lock hold is maintained across a multi-minute repair window: either (a) require the GHA-schedule-disable path exclusively (simpler, no heartbeat needed), or (b) document that the repair process must run a heartbeat loop calling `renew_pass_lock` at intervals < `lock_lease_secs // 3` and raise an abort if `ReconcileLockLost` is signaled.
findings.finding: The plan proposes 'holding the reconciler pass-lock `refs/reconciler/lock` for the duration' of the repair window, but `refs/reconciler/lock` is a leased CAS lock (ADR 0031 C2, default lease 120s). A multi-batch repair of 2422 tickets at ≤200/batch with ≥30s inter-batch waits takes at minimum ~6 minutes (12 batches), which exceeds one 120s lease interval. Without a lease heartbeat/renew loop in the repair process, the lock becomes steal-able mid-repair and the reconciler could acquire the lock and interleave writes. The plan says 'hold for the duration' but specifies no heartbeat mechanism; `renew_pass_lock` / `_Heartbeat` exist in the codebase but are not called out.
findings.location: ## Safety — 'Reconciler pause (REAL mechanism …) pause by … (b) holding the reconciler pass-lock `refs/reconciler/lock` (ADR 0031) for the duration'
findings.suggested_fix: Given the operational complexity of heartbeating a repair script, recommend the GHA-schedule-disable path as the primary mechanism and demote the pass-lock hold to an additional safety measure (not the sole option). Add to AC3: 'if the pass-lock path is chosen, the repair process must heartbeat the lock at ≤40s intervals and abort on lease-lost.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the marker sweep mechanism: either (a) fsck --repair auto-deletes all .a3-repaired markers at the end of the run when the post-run fsck scan confirms zero faults, or (b) a separate `fsck --sweep-markers` sub-command is documented; in either case, add a condition that markers are NOT swept if any unresolved faults remain.
findings.finding: The .a3-repaired marker sweep is underspecified: 'markers are swept once the whole store reaches zero faults' does not name who triggers the sweep, what command performs it, or whether it is automatic (at the end of the final fsck --repair run) or a separate manual step, leaving the lifecycle incomplete.
findings.location: Safety section — 'markers are swept once the whole store reaches zero faults'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the fsck --repair flow: after the final batch completes, re-run fsck in read-only mode; if the result is zero faults, automatically delete all .a3-repaired markers; if faults remain, preserve markers and report. Document this in the AC.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a synthetic-fixture test that asserts `fsck --repair --dry-run` produces ZERO file mutations (no writes, no renames, no marker creation) on a fixture containing all three fault types — making the dry-run safety contract machine-verified, not just human-reviewed.
findings.finding: The dry-run flag's safety contract ('writes nothing') has no automated test — only a human-review gate. A regression where `--dry-run` silently performs writes would pass the test suite and corrupt live data before the human gate catches it.
findings.location: AC line 1: 'fsck --repair --dry-run prints per-ticket before/after + dispositions, writes nothing'; AC line 5 (test AC)
findings.suggested_fix: In the fixture test for each fault class, capture the filesystem state before and after running `--dry-run`, assert the state is byte-for-byte identical (no new files, no modified files, no `.a3-repaired` markers created), and assert the printed diff/disposition output is non-empty (proving the path was reached).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace 'SIGNATURE-mirror' in the HUMAN TRIAGE disposition list with the actual KNOWN_EVENT_TYPES name 'SIGNATURE', and confirm the disposition policy covers every type in KNOWN_EVENT_TYPES exactly once (AUTO-RECOVER or HUMAN TRIAGE).
findings.finding: 'SIGNATURE-mirror' is listed as a HUMAN TRIAGE event type in the orphan disposition policy, but this name does not exist in KNOWN_EVENT_TYPES (which contains 'SIGNATURE', not 'SIGNATURE-mirror'). An executor implementing the fsck --repair orphan dispatcher cannot match this phantom type to any real on-disk event file.
findings.location: ## Orphan disposition policy — HUMAN TRIAGE list
findings.suggested_fix: Change 'SIGNATURE-mirror' → 'SIGNATURE'. Then verify the union of AUTO-RECOVER types ∪ HUMAN TRIAGE types == KNOWN_EVENT_TYPES − {CREATE, SNAPSHOT, ARCHIVED, REVERT, BRIDGE_ALERT, WORKFLOW_RUN, WORKFLOW_STEP} (non-orphanable types), with no phantom names.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a hard write-exclusion mechanism for manual writers during the repair window (e.g., a repo-level advisory lock file that the repair process creates and fsck/writer commands check, or an explicit 'maintenance mode' ref that writer commands refuse to proceed past) so that 'manual writers must hold off' is enforced, not just documented.
findings.finding: The reconciler is hard-paused but manual writers are only 'held off' via documentation/social contract, creating a TOCTOU window: the repair process reads a snapshot, a concurrent manual writer updates that same snapshot, then the repair's os.rename(tmp, original) silently overwrites the newer legitimate write.
findings.location: Safety section — 'document that manual writers must hold off during the window' / 'per-snapshot replace is atomic (write <snap>.tmp, then os.rename over the old)'
findings.suggested_fix: Introduce a `refs/repair/lock` (analogous to `refs/reconciler/lock`) that the repair process holds for the duration; have all writer paths (including the CLI's ticket-write commands) check for this ref and refuse or wait. Document the lock in the same ADR that covers the reconciler lock.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify that fsck --repair re-reads and re-evaluates each un-marked ticket from its current on-disk state (not a cached fault list) before applying a fix, so that a ticket whose snapshot is already correct is a no-op even without a marker; OR make the marker write atomic with the snapshot rename (e.g., write both into a single git commit or use a tmp marker renamed into place before the snapshot rename).
findings.finding: There is a crash-recovery gap in the idempotency guarantee: if the process exits after os.rename(snap.tmp, snap) succeeds but before the .a3-repaired marker is written, the ticket has no marker; on re-run the repair is re-applied to an already-repaired snapshot, and the plan does not specify whether the re-run re-scans the snapshot for the fault first or reuses a cached fault list, leaving ambiguous whether double-repair is safe.
findings.location: Safety section — 'a per-ticket repaired marker = a side-file written after that ticket's atomic per-snapshot rewrite'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit post-rollback state assertion to the Safety section: enumerate which files are present/absent after `git reset --hard pre-a3-remediation` (snapshots restored to pre-remediation content, `.a3-repaired` markers absent, `.retired` files at pre-tag state), and clarify the required ordering of A1 deployment relative to when the backup tag must be created.
findings.finding: The plan does not explicitly assert the store's schema/content STATE after a rollback — it documents the rollback command and mandates rehearsal, but does not state what the store looks like post-reset (e.g., that all rewritten snapshots are fully restored to their broken-but-consistent pre-remediation content, that .a3-repaired markers are fully absent, and that .retired renames from the repair are undone). Per T11, 'rollback exits 0' is insufficient; the post-rollback state must be affirmatively described.
findings.location: Safety section — 'Rollback: git -C <tracker> reset --hard pre-a3-remediation && git push --force-with-lease'; AC line 2 'rollback documented AND rehearsed on a fixture'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the failure handling for `.a3-repaired` marker writes: either (a) treat a marker-write failure as a ticket-level failure (log+skip the marker, accept re-processing on re-run and rely on idempotency of each repair type), or (b) write the marker atomically (tmp+rename) and treat its failure as a fatal per-ticket error.
findings.finding: The `.a3-repaired` marker write failure after a successful per-ticket repair is not addressed: if the marker file write fails (e.g. disk full, permission error), the ticket is already repaired on disk but the marker is absent, so a re-run will re-process the ticket — potentially double-applying the repair (retiring files already retired, or re-rebuilding an already-correct snapshot).
findings.location: ## Safety — Idempotent + resumable paragraph
findings.suggested_fix: Write the marker atomically (`.a3-repaired.tmp` → `.a3-repaired`) and document that per-repair idempotency guards (the `.retired` existence check for SNAPSHOT_INCONSISTENT; snapshot round-trip equality for ORPHAN_EVENT) make re-processing safe even without the marker.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving command for the rollback rehearsal in AC2 (e.g., a pytest fixture that tags a test-store, corrupts it, runs `git reset --hard <tag>`, and asserts `rebar fsck` returns 0 faults on the restored state).
findings.finding: AC2 requires the rollback be 'rehearsed on a fixture' but provides no proving command or observable artifact — an agent cannot verify rehearsal occurred without a concrete check (e.g., a test that applies the backup tag, runs `git reset --hard`, and asserts the fixture store state matches the pre-tag snapshot).
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2: 'Pre-remediation backup tag created; rollback documented AND rehearsed on a fixture.'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'rehearsed on a fixture' with: 'a pytest test (e.g. `tests/test_a3_rollback.py`) applies the backup tag to a synthetic store, executes `git reset --hard pre-a3-remediation`, and asserts `rebar fsck` reports 0 faults — CI must be green on this test before the live run proceeds.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the concrete proving command for 'no in-flight pass' in AC3 (e.g., `git ls-remote <tracker> refs/reconciler/lock` returns empty, AND the most-recent GHA workflow run for `reconcile-bridge.yml` is in a terminal state).
findings.finding: AC3 requires 'verified no in-flight pass' before starting but specifies no concrete mechanism or command to make that determination, leaving an agent unable to satisfy the criterion without guessing.
findings.location: Safety section ('verify no pass is mid-flight before starting') and AC3 ('verified no in-flight pass').
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC3: 'Proven by: `git ls-remote <tracker> refs/reconciler/lock` is empty AND `gh run list --workflow=reconcile-bridge.yml --limit=1 --json status -q .[][].status` equals `completed`; both checks logged in the ops record before first write.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit proving command to AC6 with a cardinality assertion (e.g., `rebar fsck 2>&1 | grep -cE 'SNAPSHOT_INCONSISTENT|ORPHAN_EVENT|MISSING_CREATE' == 0` or `rebar fsck --json | jq -e '.faults | length == 0'`).
findings.finding: AC6 asserts 'Live store reaches 0 UNRESOLVED faults' but provides no explicit proving command — the verification step (re-running `rebar fsck` and asserting zero faults) is implied but never stated, and the cardinality assertion is absent.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC6: 'Live store reaches 0 UNRESOLVED faults (triaged+documented orphans count as resolved); before/after counts recorded.'
findings.suggested_fix: Append to AC6: 'Proven by: `rebar fsck --json | jq -e \'[.faults[] | select(.status=="UNRESOLVED")] | length == 0\'` exits 0; output saved as `a3-final-fsck.json` in the PR artifacts.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the default implementation for the inter-batch wait in the Safety section (e.g., 'the `--repair` loop calls `time.sleep(30)` between batches automatically; no operator action required').
findings.finding: The inter-batch wait mechanism (>=30s between batches) is unspecified as automated-in-script vs. manual-operator-action — an agent must choose an implementation detail that affects both the script's design and the ops procedure.
findings.location: Safety section: 'Batched/throttled: `fsck --repair --limit=200`, wait >=30s between batches.'
findings.suggested_fix: Add: 'The wait is automated inside `fsck.py`'s repair loop via `time.sleep(30)` — no manual operator step required between batches.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a plan-body description of the rollback-rehearsal fixture (e.g., a local clone of a small synthetic tracker snapshot) and the rehearsal procedure (run repair, create tag, corrupt state, execute rollback, verify store matches pre-tag OID) so AC2's 'rehearsed on a fixture' requirement is fully covered.
findings.finding: AC2 mandates rollback be 'rehearsed on a fixture', but the plan body contains no description of what that fixture is, how the rehearsal is conducted, or where it fits in the workflow — making this criterion element unverifiable and orphaned from any described deliverable.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2; Safety section
findings.suggested_fix: In the Safety section, add a 'Rollback rehearsal' sub-bullet: 'Before the live run, rehearse on a synthetic fixture: (1) create a minimal tracker clone with injected faults, (2) run fsck --repair, (3) tag pre-a3-remediation, (4) run rollback commands, (5) assert store OID matches the tag. This satisfies AC2.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Persist the orphan-event disposition taxonomy (AUTO-RECOVER vs HUMAN TRIAGE categories + rationale) in an ADR or ops runbook referenced from the Scope/AC, not only in the PR description.
findings.finding: The orphan-event disposition policy — which event types are AUTO-RECOVER vs HUMAN TRIAGE and the rationale (additive/safe vs LWW-scalar/order-unrecoverable) — is a reusable business rule captured only in this plan/PR. The spec says individual triage decisions go in 'the PR / a linked ops doc', but doesn't require the taxonomy itself to be persisted in an ADR, CLAUDE.md, or ops runbook. A future operator encountering new orphan faults will have no authoritative reference.
findings.location: Orphan disposition policy section
findings.suggested_fix: Add a line to the Scope section: 'docs/ops/orphan-disposition-policy.md (new) — records the AUTO-RECOVER / HUMAN-TRIAGE taxonomy and rationale; referenced from CLAUDE.md repair section.' Add an AC bullet: '[ ] Orphan disposition policy doc merged alongside the repair.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Expose the inter-batch wait as a CLI flag (e.g., `--batch-delay-secs`, defaulting to 30) so operators can tune throttle without code changes.
findings.finding: The inter-batch throttle (30s wait between batches) is specified as a fixed literal with no CLI flag or config parameter, while the batch size is correctly exposed via --limit. An operator who needs to reduce back-pressure (e.g., on a slower store) or complete the remediation faster cannot tune the interval without editing source code.
findings.location: Safety section — 'Batched/throttled: fsck --repair --limit=200, wait >=30s between batches'
findings.suggested_fix: Add `--batch-delay-secs=30` alongside `--limit` in the CLI signature documented in the Scope section, and add it to the idempotency/batching AC bullet.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one retry (with short backoff) for transient I/O errors (EAGAIN, EIO, lock contention) in the per-ticket repair path, and distinguish transient-retry-exhausted from permanent errors in the skip log.
findings.finding: No retry/backoff specified for transient per-ticket I/O failures (e.g. filesystem rename EAGAIN, git lock contention on the tracker repo). The plan specifies log+skip on failure, so a ticket hit by a transient error is silently deferred to the next manual re-run rather than retried in-process.
findings.location: Safety section — 'Per-ticket failure = log + skip + include in the final report' / AC line 4
findings.suggested_fix: In fsck.py repair loop: wrap the os.rename (and any git write) in a retry helper (e.g. 2 attempts, 1s backoff); only log+skip after all retries exhausted; record `failure_reason: transient|permanent` in the final report.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the plan's inline `<snap>.tmp` + `os.rename` specification with an explicit directive to call `fsutil.atomic_write` (already used by compact.py:138 for the identical SNAPSHOT-write case), keeping the repair path consistent with the codebase's canonical crash-atomic write contract.
findings.finding: The Safety section specifies "write `<snap>.tmp`, then `os.rename` over the old" for the per-snapshot atomic rewrite — a manual re-implementation of `fsutil.atomic_write`, which already exists at `src/rebar/_store/fsutil.py` and is used at 10+ call-sites (compact.py:138, txn.py:251, snapshot/cache.py:123, hlc.py:137, graph/_cache.py:75, session_log.py:93, etc.) as the single canonical crash-atomic write primitive.
findings.location: Safety section — "write `<snap>.tmp`, then `os.rename` over the old"; Scope — `_commands/fsck.py (per-snapshot rewrite)`
findings.suggested_fix: In the Safety section, replace "write `<snap>.tmp`, then `os.rename` over the old" with "use `fsutil.atomic_write(snap_path, canonical_str(new_snapshot), encoding='utf-8')` (the same call compact.py:138 uses) — temp-in-same-dir + `os.replace`, crash-atomic by construction".

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to the Scope section a concrete spec for `fsck --repair` argument parsing: how `--repair`/`--dry-run`/`--limit=N` are parsed in `fsck_cli`, whether they share the existing `_scan()` path or introduce a new `_repair()` path, and what the exit-code contract is for `--repair` (repaired=0? issues-remaining=1?).
findings.finding: The plan asserts `_commands/fsck.py` already supports `--repair`, `--dry-run`, and `--limit` flags as edit targets for A3. In reality, `fsck_cli` in fsck.py accepts only `--output` (via `parse_output`) with no `--repair`, `--dry-run`, or `--limit` parsing. These are entirely new CLI additions. The Scope section lists `fsck.py` as the implementation target without specifying the argument-parsing mechanism, the exit-code contract for dry-run, or how `--limit` interacts with the existing `_scan` + `_check_snapshot` call structure. The plan's AC phrasing ('prints per-ticket before/after diff + dispositions, writes NOTHING') implies a new execution mode that does not compose trivially with the current `_scan → fsck_cli` pipeline.
findings.location: Scope section: `_commands/fsck.py (--repair/--dry-run/--limit + per-ticket marker + per-snapshot rewrite)` + AC line 1

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify what A3 specifically adds to `_commands/compact.py` beyond A1's `.retired` rename (or remove compact.py from A3's Scope if A1 fully covers it).
findings.finding: The Scope section lists '_commands/compact.py (retire-still-present path)' as an A3 edit target, but A1 (tricolour-head-ratfish) already fully owns all compact.py changes: it replaces os.remove with os.rename and adds the .retired lifecycle. A3's compact.py edit target is either a duplicate of A1's deliverable or specifies additional, currently unidentified changes — making it an ambiguous/potentially hallucinated edit target.
findings.location: Scope section: '_commands/compact.py (retire-still-present path)'
findings.suggested_fix: Remove `_commands/compact.py` from A3's Scope if the retire-still-present logic is entirely delivered by A1's os.rename change. If A3 needs additional compact.py logic (e.g. calling the retire path from the fsck repair loop), name the specific function/hunk that A3 adds.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief positive rationale for the chosen in-place batched-repair approach (e.g., 'in-place repair reuses existing write primitives and is resumable on crash; an offline-clone approach would require a force-push and loses the per-ticket atomicity guarantee of the existing lock').
findings.finding: The plan does not state a positive rationale for the chosen approach (per-ticket in-place file repair with .a3-repaired markers, batched via --limit, running directly on the live tracker working tree). The WHY of the approach — as opposed to e.g. an offline worktree clone approach — is absent. The plan states safety properties of the mechanism but not why this design was preferred over structurally different alternatives.
findings.location: ## Scope / ## Why (addresses the problem motivation, not the approach rationale)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact `gh workflow disable reconcile-bridge.yml` / `gh workflow enable reconcile-bridge.yml` invocations (and the required `actions: write` token scope) in the Safety section, OR clarify that option (b) (pass-lock hold) is the preferred operator path and option (a) requires a GHA-context runner — so the precedence is unambiguous.
findings.finding: The plan names 'disabling the reconcile-bridge.yml GHA schedule for the window' as a reconciler pause option, but does not specify the concrete CLI invocation (`gh workflow disable` / `gh workflow enable`) or confirm that the `gh` CLI's `workflow disable` subcommand is available in the remediation operator's environment (it is present in GHA runners, but the repair script runs from an operator shell, not a workflow step).
findings.location: Safety section — 'pause by (a) disabling the reconcile-bridge.yml GHA schedule for the window'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Safety section: 'Option (a) requires `gh auth login` with a token holding `actions: write` scope; invocation: `gh workflow disable .github/workflows/reconcile-bridge.yml` / `gh workflow enable ...` after repair. Preferred operator path for local execution is option (b) (pass-lock hold via `_ref_lock.acquire` / `release_pass_lock`), which requires no additional auth beyond repo push access.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
